etymology.description: semantic 牛(牛) (niú, "cow") + phonetic 玄(玄) (xuán) -> leading an ox by hand
etymology.interpretation: In the character 牽 (qiān, "to lead"), 牛(牛) (niú, "cow") represents the act of leading or guiding a cow, which was a common agricultural practice. The phonetic 玄(玄) (xuán) provides the pronunciation. Together, they form the concept of leading an animal, specifically an ox.
etymology.simplification: 牽 (qiān) was simplified by removing 冖(冖) (mì, "cover; crown") to create 牵 (qiān).

examples.chinese: 小女孩牵着狗在公园里散步(小女孩牽著狗在公園裡散步)。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo nǚhái qiān zhe gǒu zài gōngyuán lǐ sànbù.
examples.english: The little girl is leading the dog for a walk in the park.

examples.chinese: 老人牵着孩子的手，慢慢走过马路(老人牽著孩子的手，慢慢走過馬路)。
examples.pinyin: Lǎorén qiān zhe háizi de shǒu, mànmàn zǒu guò mǎlù.
examples.english: The old man is leading the child by the hand, slowly crossing the street.

examples.chinese: 她牵着马走向草地(她牽著馬走向草地)。
examples.pinyin: Tā qiān zhe mǎ zǒu xiàng cǎodì.
examples.english: She is leading the horse towards the meadow.
